utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: kayla ward , date of birth , 4/28/07 . mrn 3-8-4-9-2-0 . she's here for a new visit with her mother for acne located on the face , which started about two years ago and is present most every day . she has been using persa-gel and washing regularly , which is somewhat helpful . there are no associated symptoms including itching , bleeding , or pain . no additional past medical history . she lives with her parents and sister . they have a dog , bird , and bunnies . she is in 7th grade . she plays basketball and volleyball and tap . she wears sunscreen in the summer , spf 30 . no additional family history . hi kayla , i'm dr. juan price . i hear you are starting to get some acne on the face . how about the chest and back ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's not too bad .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so , it's not bad on the chest or back . you've used some over the counter items like washes and persa-gel ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do those seem to be helping ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , i think so , a little bit .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good . what's your skin care routine like now ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: do you wan na know , like , the things i currently use ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes . what do you do for your acne in the morning ? and then what do you do at nighttime ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i wash my face , more like i wipe it down in the morning . then at night i use an elf facial cleanser called the super clarity cleanser . i finish with a toner and then the persa-gel .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: when you say , " wipe your face in the morning , " do you use a product or just water ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm , just water and a washcloth , really . if i feel really greasy , sometimes i'll use the elf cleanser in the morning , too .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and is today a good day , bad day , or an average day for you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm , i would say it's probably a good day for me , of course , since i'm here , right ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: acne is always good when you come to see the doctor . do you find that your acne flares with your periods ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , not really .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and do you get a regular period ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yup .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how long have you been getting a regular period ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm , i think about two years .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: the biggest flare , probably , was when she started school sports in the fall , just with all the sweating .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yup , that will do it . is there anything else that you've noticed , mrs. ward ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no . kayla really has been doing a good job with the facial care regimen . it just does n't seem to help as much as we wanted .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: got it . okay . well , let's take a look then . full exam is performed today , except for under the underwear and under the bra . multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities . scattered skin colored papules . open and closed comedones . and erythmateous papules on the face , primarily on the forehead and with also some on the central cheeks and chin . the chest and back are relatively spared . and the remainder of the examination is normal . so , what i'm seeing from your exam today is mild to moderate acne , mostly comodonal with small inflammatory component .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , so is there anything we can do to help it ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes . i would like to start with a topical therapy first . every morning , you will wash your face with a mild cleanser then use a moisturizer labeled , " noncomedogenic , " with sunscreen spf 30 or higher . this means it wo n't clog your pores . now , in the evening , wash your face with the same cleanser and allow it to dry . apply adapalene , 0.1 % cream , in a thin layer to the areas you generally get acne . i want you to start off using this a few nights a week and slowly work up to using it every night . if it is ... excuse me , if it is very expensive or not covered by insurance , you can try different gel over the counter . you can follow that with clean and clear persa-gel in a thin layer , or where you generally get acne . and then a noncomedogenic moisturizer . you're atopic retinoid will cause some sensitivity , so you will need to wear sunscreen when you are outside . it may also cause some dryness or irritation .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , i can do that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you also have multiple benign moles on your arms , legs , back and abdomen . this means they all look normal with no worrisome features . we will see if you have any progress over the next six months and follow up at that time .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , that sounds good . thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do you have any questions for me ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i do n't think so .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . if you have questions or concerns before your next visit , please call the office .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thank you , doctor , we will .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 15-year-old female presenting for a new acne evaluation.
The patient's acne started two years ago and is present daily.
The patient reports no itching, bleeding, or pain associated with her acne.
The patient lives with parents and a sister, has pets, is in 7th grade, and participates in sports.
The doctor asks if the patient has acne on her chest or back.
The patient reports her acne is not severe on her chest or back.
The doctor asks if the patient has used over-the-counter washes and Persa-Gel.
The doctor asks if the current over-the-counter treatments are helping.
The patient reports that her current treatments are helping a little bit.
The doctor asks about the patient's current skin care routine.
The doctor asks for specific details of the patient's morning and nighttime acne routine.
The patient wipes her face in the morning, and at night uses e.l.f. SuperClarify Cleanser, toner, and Persa-Gel.
The doctor asks if the patient uses a product or just water to wipe her face in the morning.
The patient uses water and a washcloth in the morning, occasionally using the e.l.f. cleanser if her skin feels greasy.
The doctor asks if today is a good, bad, or average day for the patient's acne.
The patient reports today is a good day for her acne.
The doctor asks if the patient's acne flares with her menstrual periods.
The patient reports her acne does not flare with her periods.
The doctor asks if the patient has regular periods.
The patient confirms she has regular periods.
The doctor asks how long the patient has been having regular periods.
The patient has had regular periods for about two years.
The patient notes her acne flares most significantly during school sports in the fall due to sweating.
Physical exam shows multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities, and mild to moderate comedonal acne on the face.
The patient asks if there is anything that can be done to help her acne.
The doctor recommends a morning routine of mild cleanser and non-comedogenic moisturizer with SPF 30, and an evening routine of mild cleanser and topical retinoid (Adapalene 0.1% gel).
The doctor reassures the patient that her moles are benign and normal, and plans to monitor them in six months.
The doctor schedules a follow-up visit in six months.
The doctor asks if the patient has any further questions.
